description: Mass spectral data for educational Mass Spectrometry simulator. Add compounds following the schema below. Peak ion types: molecular, base, fragment, isotope, mplus1, mplus2.

schema.name: string
keyFeatures: array of strings for learning highlights

peaks.label: string (short label for chart)
peaks.explanation: string (pedagogical explanation)

compounds.name: Boron
compounds.description: Boron has two stable isotopes: ¹⁰B and ¹¹B. This element beautifully demonstrates how mass spectra reveal isotopic composition. The more abundant ¹¹B peak is assigned 100% and ¹⁰B appears at about 24.8%.
keyFeatures: ¹¹B is the more abundant isotope
Used to calculate relative atomic mass

peaks.label: ¹⁰B
peaks.explanation: The lighter isotope of boron (¹⁰B). Natural abundance is 19.8%. Relative to the base peak (¹¹B = 100%), this peak appears at ~24.8%. Mass = 10 amu.

peaks.label: ¹¹B (base)
peaks.explanation: The more abundant isotope of boron (¹¹B). Natural abundance is 80.2%. This is the tallest peak (base peak = 100%). Mass = 11 amu. Relative atomic mass of B ≈ 10.8.

compounds.name: Silicon
compounds.description: Silicon has three stable isotopes: ²⁸Si (92.2%), ²⁹Si (4.7%), and ³⁰Si (3.1%). This gives a mass spectrum with three distinct peaks, all shifted by 1 mass unit from each other.

peaks.label: ²⁸Si
peaks.explanation: The most abundant silicon isotope (92.2%). Assigned 100% as base peak. Contributes most to the relative atomic mass calculation.

peaks.label: ²⁹Si
peaks.explanation: The second silicon isotope (4.7% natural abundance). Appears at m/z = 29, just 5.1% relative to the base peak at m/z = 28.

peaks.label: ³⁰Si
peaks.explanation: The third silicon isotope (3.1% natural abundance). Smallest of the three peaks at m/z = 30. Relative atomic mass of Si = (0.922×28) + (0.047×29) + (0.031×30) = 28.1.

compounds.name: Germanium
compounds.description: Germanium has five stable isotopes spanning m/z 70–76. This complex isotope pattern is a classic example used in CAPE/A-level chemistry to practise Ar calculations. The largest peak (³⁷Ge) is at m/z 74.

peaks.label: ⁷⁰Ge
peaks.explanation: ⁷⁰Ge has a natural abundance of 20.5%. Expressed relative to the base peak (⁷⁴Ge = 100%), this peak appears at ~56.2%.

peaks.label: ⁷²Ge
peaks.explanation: ⁷²Ge has a natural abundance of 27.4%. Second most abundant isotope of germanium.

peaks.label: ⁷³Ge
peaks.explanation: ⁷³Ge has a natural abundance of 7.8%. Note there is no m/z = 71 peak — only even-mass isotopes dominate here.

peaks.label: ⁷⁴Ge (base)
peaks.explanation: ⁷⁴Ge is the most abundant isotope (36.5%), so it is the base peak at 100%. Ar = (20.5×70 + 27.4×72 + 7.8×73 + 36.5×74 + 7.8×76) / 100 = 72.6.

peaks.label: ⁷⁶Ge
peaks.explanation: ⁷⁶Ge has a natural abundance of 7.8%, equal to ⁷³Ge. Note there is no m/z = 75 peak in this pattern.

compounds.name: Chlorine Gas (Cl₂)
compounds.description: The mass spectrum of Cl₂ shows three molecular ion peaks at m/z 70, 72, and 74 in a 9:6:1 ratio, arising from the combinations of ³⁵Cl and ³⁷Cl isotopes. This is the classic demonstration of the M, M+2, M+4 pattern for two halogen atoms.

peaks.label: ³⁵Cl⁺
peaks.explanation: Atomic chlorine fragment. The singly-charged ³⁵Cl ion. Natural abundance of ³⁵Cl is 75.5%, making it the base peak in the atomic region.

peaks.label: ³⁷Cl⁺
peaks.explanation: Atomic ³⁷Cl fragment. Natural abundance 24.5%. Ratio of ³⁵Cl⁺ : ³⁷Cl⁺ ≈ 3:1, consistent with natural isotopic abundances.

peaks.label: M⁺ (³⁵Cl₂)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion with both atoms being ³⁵Cl. Probability ∝ (0.755)² = 0.570. This gives the largest molecular ion peak at m/z = 70. Ratio M:M+2:M+4 = 9:6:1.

peaks.label: M+2 (³⁵Cl³⁷Cl)
peaks.explanation: M+2 peak: one ³⁵Cl and one ³⁷Cl atom. Can be ³⁵Cl-³⁷Cl or ³⁷Cl-³⁵Cl, so probability ∝ 2(0.755)(0.245) = 0.370. Approximately 6/9 × 90% ≈ 60%.

peaks.label: M+4 (³⁷Cl₂)
peaks.explanation: M+4 peak: both atoms are ³⁷Cl. Probability ∝ (0.245)² = 0.060. Smallest molecular ion peak at m/z = 74. This three-peak 9:6:1 pattern is the diagnostic fingerprint of Cl₂.

compounds.name: Butane
compounds.description: Butane is a straight-chain alkane. Its spectrum shows systematic fragmentation with the loss of successive CH₂ groups (Δm/z = 14). The base peak at m/z 43 corresponds to the C₃H₇⁺ ion. Notable peaks at m/z 29 (C₂H₅⁺) and 15 (CH₃⁺) confirm the carbon chain.

peaks.label: M⁺ (58)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion. Loss of one electron from butane. Relative atomic mass = 4(12) + 10(1) = 58. Molecular ions of alkanes are generally weak due to easy fragmentation.

peaks.label: M−1
peaks.explanation: Loss of a hydrogen atom from the molecular ion (M−1). Weak peak. C₄H₉⁺ = butyl cation.

peaks.label: C₃H₇⁺ (base)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. Formed by loss of a CH₃ radical (mass 15) from the molecular ion: [C₄H₁₀]⁺• → C₃H₇⁺ + CH₃•. The n-propyl cation (C₃H₇⁺) is relatively stable. 4(12)+7(1) = 55 — wait, 3(12)+7(1) = 43 ✓.

peaks.label: C₃H₆⁺•
peaks.explanation: Loss of H• from the C₃H₇⁺ ion or rearrangement product. m/z = 3(12)+6(1) = 42.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Formed by further fragmentation. The allyl cation C₃H₅⁺ is stabilized by resonance.

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Ethyl cation formed by loss of a C₂H₅ radical from the molecular ion, or fragmentation of the C₃H₇⁺ ion. m/z = 2(12)+5(1) = 29. Note the pattern: m/z 43 (−14) → m/z 29.

peaks.label: C₂H₄⁺•
peaks.explanation: Formed by loss of H from C₂H₅⁺ or rearrangement. Could also be CO⁺ in oxygenated compounds.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation product. m/z = 2(12)+3(1) = 27.

peaks.label: CH₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Methyl cation formed by extensive fragmentation. m/z = 12+3(1) = 15. Presence confirms a methyl group in the molecule.

compounds.name: Pentane
compounds.description: Pentane (n-pentane) shows the classic unbranched alkane fragmentation: loss of CH₃ (M−15), C₂H₅ (M−29), C₃H₇ (M−43) produces peaks at m/z 57, 43, 29. The base peak is at m/z 43 (C₃H₇⁺). Compare with its branched isomer 2-methylbutane.

peaks.label: M⁺ (72)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion of pentane. m/z = 5(12)+12(1) = 72. Present but not the base peak — alkane molecular ions fragment readily. M+1 peak at m/z 73 should be ~5.5% of M⁺ (5 carbons × 1.1%).

peaks.label: M+1
peaks.explanation: M+1 peak due to ¹³C. With 5 carbon atoms, n = 5, so M+1 = 5 × 1.1% = 5.5% of M⁺ abundance (here 22% × 5.5% ≈ 1.2%). Use formula: n = (M+1 abundance / M⁺ abundance) × (100/1.1).

peaks.label: M−15 (C₄H₉⁺)
peaks.explanation: Loss of CH₃ radical from the molecular ion. M − 15 = 72 − 15 = 57. Butyl cation.

peaks.label: C₃H₇⁺ (base)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. Loss of C₂H₅ radical (mass 29) from the molecular ion, or further fragmentation of C₄H₉⁺. m/z = 43. Most stable among the fragment ions.

peaks.label: C₃H₆⁺•
peaks.explanation: Loss of H• from C₃H₇⁺ or rearrangement. m/z = 42.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: The resonance-stabilized allyl cation. m/z = 41.

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Loss of C₃H₇ radical (mass 43) from the molecular ion. m/z = 29. Strong peak in pentane spectrum.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Vinyl cation from further fragmentation. m/z = 2(12)+3(1) = 27.

peaks.label: CH₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Methyl cation. Present in all straight-chain alkanes as a minor fragment.

compounds.name: 2-Methylbutane
compounds.description: 2-Methylbutane is a branched isomer of pentane with the same molecular formula (C₅H₁₂) and molecular mass (72). Despite having the same M⁺, the fragmentation pattern differs markedly. The branched structure leads to cleavage at the branch point, producing an unusually strong peak at m/z 57 compared to pentane.
keyFeatures: Stronger M−15 peak (m/z 57) due to branching
Fragmentation favours cleavage at branch point
Secondary carbocation at branch is more stable

peaks.label: M⁺ (72)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion. Same m/z = 72 as pentane — these isomers cannot be distinguished by M⁺ alone. Look at the fragmentation pattern instead!

peaks.label: M−15 (C₄H₉⁺)
peaks.fragmentLabel: Butyl cation C₄H₉⁺ (m/z 57) — stronger than in pentane!
peaks.explanation: Loss of CH₃ radical from the branch point. In 2-methylbutane, cleavage at the branch point gives a secondary carbocation (2-butyl or t-butyl type), which is more stable than in n-pentane. Hence m/z 57 is MUCH STRONGER here (~39%) than in pentane (~6%). This is the key difference between the two isomers.

peaks.label: C₃H₇⁺ (base)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. Same m/z as pentane's base peak, but the ion here can be the isopropyl cation [(CH₃)₂CH]⁺, which is a secondary carbocation — more stable than the primary propyl cation in pentane.

peaks.label: C₃H₆⁺•
peaks.explanation: Loss of H• from C₃H₇⁺ or rearrangement product. m/z = 42.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Allyl cation fragment. m/z = 41. Less intense than in n-pentane.

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Ethyl cation. m/z = 29. Still significant but different relative intensity to n-pentane.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation product. m/z = 27.

peaks.label: CH₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Methyl cation. Slightly stronger than in n-pentane due to the extra methyl branch in 2-methylbutane.

compounds.name: Propene
compounds.description: Propene is the simplest alkene with a visible molecular ion. The π electrons are more easily removed than σ electrons, so the molecular ion is relatively abundant. Loss of H gives the allyl cation (m/z 41), stabilized by resonance across the double bond.
keyFeatures: π electrons easier to ionize → strong molecular ion
Strong M⁺ peak compared to alkanes
Fragmentation at C=C leads to allylic ions

peaks.label: M⁺ (42)
peaks.explanation: Strong molecular ion. The π electrons of the double bond are more easily removed, giving a relatively abundant M⁺. m/z = 3(12)+6(1) = 42. This is more stable than an alkane radical cation.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺ (base)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. Loss of H• from the molecular ion gives the resonance-stabilized allyl cation [CH₂=CH−CH₂ ↔ •CH₂−CH=CH₂]⁺. The delocalization of the positive charge makes this the most stable fragment, hence the base peak.

peaks.label: C₃H₄⁺•
peaks.explanation: Further loss of H from the allyl cation. Could be propyne (CH≡C−CH₃)⁺• or allene (CH₂=C=CH₂)⁺•. m/z = 40.

peaks.label: C₃H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: The cyclopropenyl cation is the simplest aromatic cation (2 π electrons). Very stable and gives a prominent peak at m/z = 39. This peak is characteristic of C₃ hydrocarbons.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Vinyl cation from C−C bond cleavage. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: But-1-ene
compounds.description: But-1-ene has the same molecular formula and mass as cyclobutane (C₄H₈, M=56), illustrating how MS alone sometimes cannot distinguish structural isomers. The dominant fragmentation gives the allyl cation (m/z 41) by β-cleavage adjacent to the double bond.

peaks.label: M⁺ (56)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 4(12)+8(1) = 56. The π electrons of the C=C bond are ionized preferentially. Fairly abundant M⁺ compared to saturated alkanes of similar mass.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺ (base)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. β-cleavage: the C−C bond β to the double bond cleaves, releasing CH₃• and generating the allyl cation (CH₂=CH−CH₂⁺). The allyl cation is resonance-stabilized. m/z = 3(12)+5(1) = 41.

peaks.label: C₃H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Cyclopropenyl cation from further loss of H₂ from the allyl cation. m/z = 39. Characteristic of C₃ fragments.

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Ethyl cation from C−C cleavage in the chain. m/z = 29.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Vinyl cation. A prominent fragment in alkene spectra. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: Benzene
compounds.description: Benzene has an exceptionally stable molecular ion due to the delocalized aromatic π system. The molecular ion IS the base peak. There is almost no fragmentation, making it the simplest aromatic spectrum to recognize. The M+1 peak at m/z 79 is ~6.6% of M⁺ (6 carbons × 1.1%).
keyFeatures: Peaks at m/z 51, 50 from ring fragmentation

peaks.label: M⁺ (78) = base
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK = MOLECULAR ION. The aromatic π system delocalizes the positive charge over the entire ring, making the molecular ion exceptionally stable. Very little fragmentation occurs. This is a diagnostic feature of benzene. m/z = 6(12)+6(1) = 78.

peaks.label: M+1 (79)
peaks.explanation: M+1 PEAK. With 6 carbon atoms: M+1% = 6 × 1.1% = 6.6% of M⁺ abundance. From n = (M+1/M⁺) × (100/1.1) = (6.6/100) × (100/1.1) ≈ 6 carbon atoms. This confirms the molecular formula.

peaks.label: C₆H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Loss of H• from the molecular ion gives the phenyl cation [C₆H₅]⁺. m/z = 6(12)+5(1) = 77. Significant peak in all benzene-ring compounds.

peaks.label: C₄H₄⁺•
peaks.explanation: Further ring fragmentation. m/z = 4(12)+4(1) = 52. Arises from opening and rearrangement of the ring.

peaks.label: C₄H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Characteristic aromatic fragment at m/z = 51. Formed by ring contraction and loss of C₂H₂ units. m/z = 4(12)+3(1) = 51.

peaks.label: C₄H₂⁺•
peaks.explanation: Another aromatic ring fragmentation product. m/z = 4(12)+2(1) = 50. The pattern of peaks at 50, 51, 52 is characteristic of the benzene ring system.

compounds.name: Methylbenzene (Toluene)
compounds.description: Methylbenzene shows the characteristic loss of a CH₃ group followed by cyclization to the highly stable tropylium cation [C₇H₇]⁺ at m/z 91, which IS the base peak. The tropylium cation is a 6π aromatic cation (cyclic, planar, 4n+2 electrons). This pattern — large M⁺ and large m/z 91 — is the fingerprint of all alkylbenzenes.
keyFeatures: Tropylium cation [C₇H₇]⁺ at m/z 91 = base peak
Loss of H from tropylium gives m/z 90

peaks.label: M⁺ (92)
peaks.explanation: Strong molecular ion at m/z = 7(12)+8(1) = 92. The aromatic ring stabilizes the radical cation. Moderately strong M⁺ compared to aliphatic compounds.

peaks.label: Tropylium (base, 91)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. Loss of H• from M⁺ gives the tropylium cation [C₇H₇]⁺. This is a cyclic, planar, 6π-electron aromatic cation (cycloheptatrienyl). Its exceptional stability (aromaticity!) makes it the dominant fragment. m/z = 7(12)+7(1) = 91. This peak is the SIGNATURE of alkylbenzenes.

peaks.label: C₅H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Loss of C₂H₂ (acetylene) from the tropylium cation: [C₇H₇]⁺ → [C₅H₅]⁺ + C₂H₂. The cyclopentadienyl cation is also aromatic-like. m/z = 5(12)+5(1) = 65.

peaks.label: C₅H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation from C₅H₅⁺ by loss of H₂. m/z = 63.

peaks.label: C₄H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Characteristic benzene-ring fragment at m/z = 51. Common to all aromatic compounds.

peaks.label: C₃H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Cyclopropenyl cation, the simplest aromatic cation, formed by further ring fragmentation. m/z = 39.

compounds.name: Ethanol
compounds.description: Ethanol illustrates typical alcohol fragmentation. The molecular ion at m/z 46 is weak (alcohols fragment readily). The base peak at m/z 31 is the hydroxymethyl cation [CH₂OH]⁺ — a characteristic of primary alcohols. The peak at m/z 45 (M−1) indicates loss of H, while m/z 29 could be [CHO]⁺ or [C₂H₅]⁺.
keyFeatures: Base peak [CH₂OH]⁺ at m/z 31 — diagnostic for primary alcohols
M−1 peak at m/z 45 (loss of H)

peaks.label: M⁺ (46)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion. m/z = 2(12)+6(1)+16 = 46. Weak peak — alcohols have easily broken C−O and C−C bonds, leading to extensive fragmentation.

peaks.label: M−1 (45)
peaks.explanation: Loss of H• from the molecular ion. [C₂H₅OH]⁺• → [CH₃CHOH]⁺ + H•. The resulting oxocarbenium ion [CH₃CHOH]⁺ is stabilized by the lone pair on oxygen (α-cleavage). m/z = 45.

peaks.label: CH₂OH⁺ (base, 31)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: the C−C bond adjacent (α) to the OH-bearing carbon breaks, giving [CH₂=OH]⁺. This is the hydroxymethyl (or methoxymethyl) cation. m/z = 12+2(1)+16+1 = 31. This peak at m/z = 31 is the DIAGNOSTIC PEAK for primary alcohols (R-CH₂OH → [CH₂OH]⁺).

peaks.label: m/z 29
peaks.explanation: Two possible ions overlap at m/z 29: the formyl cation [CHO]⁺ (from oxidation-type rearrangement) and the ethyl cation [C₂H₅]⁺ (from C−OH bond cleavage). Both have m/z = 29. High-resolution MS would separate them.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Vinyl cation formed by dehydration and further fragmentation. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: Propan-1-ol
compounds.description: Propan-1-ol, a primary alcohol, shows the characteristic primary alcohol base peak at m/z 31 ([CH₂OH]⁺). The M⁺ at m/z 60 is very weak — common for alcohols. The peak at m/z 59 (M−1) shows loss of H. Compare with its isomer propan-2-ol to understand how MS distinguishes structural isomers.
keyFeatures: Compare with propan-2-ol (same M⁺, different fragmentation)
Dehydration peak at m/z 42 (loss of H₂O, 18)

peaks.label: M⁺ (60)
peaks.explanation: Very weak molecular ion at m/z = 3(12)+8(1)+16 = 60. Alcohols have notoriously weak M⁺ peaks due to rapid α-cleavage. SAME M⁺ as propan-2-ol — look at the fragmentation to distinguish them!

peaks.label: M−1 (59)
peaks.explanation: Loss of H from the molecular ion. m/z = 59.

peaks.label: M−18 (42)
peaks.explanation: Loss of water (H₂O, mass 18) from M⁺: 60 − 18 = 42. Dehydration is very common in alcohol spectra. The resulting propene radical cation [CH₃CH=CH₂]⁺• appears at m/z = 42.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Loss of H from [C₃H₆]⁺• to give the allyl cation. m/z = 41.

peaks.label: CH₂OH⁺ (base, 31)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-cleavage at the C-1 to C-2 bond: [C₃H₇OH]⁺• → [CH₂=OH]⁺ + C₂H₅•. The hydroxymethyl cation at m/z = 31 is the FINGERPRINT OF PRIMARY ALCOHOLS. Same as in ethanol. Propan-2-ol (a secondary alcohol) does NOT show this as the base peak.

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Ethyl cation formed by α-cleavage on the other side: [C₃H₇OH]⁺• → [C₂H₅]⁺ + •CH₂OH. m/z = 29.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: Propan-2-ol
compounds.description: Propan-2-ol is a SECONDARY alcohol. Although it has the same molecular formula (C₃H₈O) and M⁺ (60) as propan-1-ol, the fragmentation is completely different. The base peak at m/z 45 (loss of CH₃) is characteristic of secondary alcohols (α-cleavage gives a larger, more substituted oxocarbenium ion). The m/z 31 peak that dominates propan-1-ol is ABSENT here.
keyFeatures: Base peak m/z 45 (loss of CH₃) — secondary alcohol fingerprint
α-cleavage favours loss of largest alkyl group

peaks.label: M⁺ (60)
peaks.explanation: Very weak molecular ion, m/z = 60 — same as propan-1-ol. Cannot distinguish the isomers from M⁺ alone! The fragmentation patterns are the key diagnostic.

peaks.label: [CH₃CHOH]⁺ (base, 45)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: loss of CH₃• from the molecular ion — [CH₃)₂CHOH]⁺• → [CH₃CHOH]⁺ + CH₃•. The α-cleavage rule: loss of the LARGEST group adjacent to the OH carbon is favoured (gives most stable carbocation). m/z = 12+4(1)+12+1+16+1 = 45? Actually [CH₃CHOH]⁺ = CH₃ + CH + OH = 12+3+12+1+16+1 = 45. This SECONDARY alcohol loses CH₃ not H. Compare to the PRIMARY alcohol (propan-1-ol) where base peak was m/z 31.

peaks.label: m/z 43
peaks.explanation: m/z 43 could be the isopropyl cation (CH₃)₂CH⁺ from loss of OH•, or the acetyl cation CH₃CO⁺ from dehydrogenation. m/z = 43.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Allyl/propenyl cation from further fragmentation. m/z = 41.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation to vinyl cation. m/z = 27. Note: NO m/z 31 peak — this conclusively identifies this compound as a SECONDARY alcohol, NOT primary.

compounds.name: Ethanal (Acetaldehyde)
compounds.description: Ethanal is the simplest aldehyde with a carbon chain. Its spectrum has a relatively strong molecular ion at m/z 44. The base peak at m/z 29 ([CHO]⁺, the formyl cation) and the peak at m/z 43 ([CH₃CO]⁺, acetyl cation) from the two possible α-cleavages. A formyl (CHO⁺) peak at m/z 29 is characteristic of aldehydes.
keyFeatures: α-cleavage on both sides of C=O

peaks.label: M⁺ (44)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 2(12)+4(1)+16 = 44. Relatively strong for an aldehyde. The C=O group lone pairs are easily ionized.

peaks.label: CH₃CO⁺ (43)
peaks.explanation: α-CLEAVAGE: loss of H• from the carbonyl side: [CH₃CHO]⁺• → [CH₃CO]⁺ + H•. The acetyl cation (acylium ion) is stabilized by resonance [CH₃C≡O]⁺. m/z = 12+3(1)+12+16 = 43.

peaks.label: CHO⁺ (base, 29)
peaks.fragmentLabel: Formyl cation [CHO]⁺ = acylium ion (base peak, m/z 29)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: loss of CH₃• from the molecular ion: [CH₃CHO]⁺• → [CHO]⁺ + CH₃•. The formyl cation [H−C≡O]⁺ is stabilized by the positive charge on carbon. m/z = 12+1+16 = 29. A CHO⁺ peak near m/z 29 is DIAGNOSTIC for aldehydes. Note: in ethanol, m/z 29 could also be C₂H₅⁺.

peaks.label: CO⁺• (28)
peaks.explanation: Loss of H from the formyl cation [CHO]⁺ → [CO]⁺ + H•. The CO⁺ radical cation at m/z = 12+16 = 28. This peak is seen in many carbonyl compounds.

peaks.label: CH₃⁺ (15)
peaks.explanation: Methyl cation formed by α-cleavage or further fragmentation. m/z = 12+3(1) = 15. Confirms the presence of a CH₃ group.

compounds.name: Propanal
compounds.description: Propanal shows the classic aldehyde fragmentation: α-cleavage next to the C=O group. The formyl cation [CHO]⁺ at m/z 29 is the base peak. Note that m/z 58 is also the molecular mass of propanone — these isomers can be distinguished because propanal shows m/z 29 (CHO⁺) prominently, while propanone does not.
keyFeatures: Base peak m/z 29 = [CHO]⁺ — diagnostic for aldehyde
Propanone has base peak at m/z 43, propanal at m/z 29
m/z 28 = CO⁺ loss from formyl cation
MS clearly distinguishes these functional isomers

peaks.label: M⁺ (58)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 3(12)+6(1)+16 = 58. SAME as propanone! Compare the fragment patterns to identify which is which. The base peaks are very different.

peaks.label: M−1 (57)
peaks.explanation: α-cleavage releasing H•: [C₂H₅CHO]⁺• → [C₂H₅CO]⁺ + H•. The propanoyl (acylium) cation. m/z = 57.

peaks.label: CHO⁺ (base, 29)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: loss of C₂H₅• (mass 29) from the molecular ion: [C₂H₅CHO]⁺• → [CHO]⁺ + C₂H₅•. The formyl cation [H−C≡O]⁺ at m/z 29 is the DIAGNOSTIC PEAK for aldehydes. Compare: propanone has NO m/z 29 CHO⁺ peak. This conclusively distinguishes propanal from propanone!

peaks.label: CO⁺• (28)
peaks.explanation: Loss of H• from [CHO]⁺. CO⁺• at m/z = 28. Characteristic of aldehydes and other carbonyl compounds.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Vinyl cation from further fragmentation of the ethyl group region. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: Propanone (Acetone)
compounds.description: Propanone is the simplest ketone and gives the textbook example of ketone fragmentation. The base peak at m/z 43 is the acetyl (acylium) cation [CH₃CO]⁺, formed by α-cleavage. Since both sides of the carbonyl are identical (both are CH₃ groups), only one type of α-cleavage fragment is seen. The molecular ion at m/z 58 is moderate.
keyFeatures: α-cleavage on both sides gives same product (symmetric ketone)
Same M⁺ as propanal — compare fragmentation!

peaks.label: M⁺ (58)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 3(12)+6(1)+16 = 58. SAME molecular mass as propanal and butane — but completely different spectra. The m/z 58 peak is moderate for a ketone.

peaks.label: CH₃CO⁺ (base, 43)
peaks.fragmentLabel: Acetyl cation [CH₃CO]⁺ — acylium ion (base peak, m/z 43)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: the C−C bond next to the C=O breaks: [CH₃COCH₃]⁺• → [CH₃CO]⁺ + CH₃•. The acetyl (acylium) cation is resonance-stabilized: [CH₃−C≡O]⁺ ↔ [CH₃⁺=C=O]. m/z = 12+3(1)+12+16 = 43. In propanone, both α-cleavages give the SAME product because both R groups are CH₃, so m/z 43 is very intense.

peaks.label: CH₃⁺ (15)
peaks.explanation: The complementary α-cleavage product: [CH₃COCH₃]⁺• → CH₃• + [CH₃CO]⁺ (which gives m/z 43), or: m/z 43 → m/z 15 + CO. The methyl cation is small but detectable. m/z = 12+3(1) = 15.

compounds.name: Butanone (MEK)
compounds.description: Butanone (butan-2-one) is an asymmetric ketone, so two different α-cleavages are possible: loss of CH₃ gives m/z 57, loss of C₂H₅ gives m/z 43. The base peak at m/z 43 ([CH₃CO]⁺) is favoured over m/z 57 ([C₂H₅CO]⁺) because the methyl group is more easily lost as a radical than the ethyl group (less stable radical).

peaks.label: M⁺ (72)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 4(12)+8(1)+16 = 72. Moderate intensity for a ketone.

peaks.label: M−15 (57)
peaks.explanation: α-CLEAVAGE: loss of CH₃• from the molecular ion: [CH₃COC₂H₅]⁺• → [C₂H₅CO]⁺ + CH₃•. This gives the propanoyl (ethyl acylium) cation at m/z = 57. Less favoured than the other α-cleavage.

peaks.label: CH₃CO⁺ (base, 43)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: loss of C₂H₅• from the molecular ion: [CH₃COC₂H₅]⁺• → [CH₃CO]⁺ + C₂H₅•. This cleavage is favoured because an ethyl radical is more stable than a methyl radical, making the C₂H₅ the preferred leaving group. m/z = 43. Same fragment as propanone base peak!

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺ (29)
peaks.explanation: Ethyl cation, the complementary fragment to [CH₃CO]⁺. Formed by C−C bond cleavage on the other side of the carbonyl. m/z = 29.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation of the ethyl cation or other C₂ fragments. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: Pentan-2-one
compounds.description: Pentan-2-one shows α-cleavage (base peak m/z 43, [CH₃CO]⁺) and also the McLafferty Rearrangement, a unique γ-hydrogen migration that gives a peak at m/z 58 (loss of 28, loss of CH₂=CH₂). This rearrangement requires a γ-hydrogen and a carbonyl — it is diagnostic for many carbonyl compounds.
keyFeatures: McLafferty rearrangement gives m/z 58 (loss of ethene, 28)
McLafferty peak = M − 28 when γ-H is available

peaks.label: M⁺ (86)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 5(12)+10(1)+16 = 86. Same M⁺ as pentan-3-one — look at the fragmentation to distinguish them!

peaks.label: M−15 (71)
peaks.explanation: α-cleavage: loss of CH₃• from the methyl side: [CH₃CO(CH₂)₂CH₃]⁺• → [OC(CH₂)₂CH₃]⁺ + CH₃•. m/z = 71.

peaks.label: McLafferty (58)
peaks.explanation: McCLAFFERTY REARRANGEMENT: A γ-hydrogen (on C-4) migrates to the carbonyl oxygen via a 6-membered cyclic transition state, simultaneously breaking the Cβ−Cγ bond. Products: [CH₃−CO=CH₂]⁺• (m/z 58, methylenol cation) + •CH₂CH₃ (radical). m/z = 58 = M − 28 (loss of C₂H₄). This rearrangement is only possible when there is a γ-hydrogen — pentan-3-one CANNOT undergo this rearrangement!

peaks.label: CH₃CO⁺ (base, 43)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: loss of n-propyl radical (C₃H₇•, mass 43) from the propyl side of the carbonyl: [CH₃CO(CH₂)₂CH₃]⁺• → [CH₃CO]⁺ + •(CH₂)₂CH₃. The acetyl cation [CH₃CO]⁺ at m/z 43. Same as butanone and propanone base peaks.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Allyl cation from fragmentation of the propyl group region. m/z = 41.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: Pentan-3-one
compounds.description: Pentan-3-one is a symmetric diethyl ketone. It shares the same molecular formula and M⁺ (86) as pentan-2-one. Its base peak at m/z 57 ([C₂H₅CO]⁺) and the strong complementary peak at m/z 29 ([C₂H₅]⁺) clearly distinguish it. Importantly, pentan-3-one CANNOT undergo the McLafferty rearrangement (no γ-hydrogen on the methyl of CH₃CO — there is no CH₃ group!).
keyFeatures: Symmetric ketone: one type of α-cleavage

peaks.label: M⁺ (86)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 86. SAME as pentan-2-one. Indistinguishable by M⁺ alone — the α-cleavage fragments are completely different!

peaks.label: C₂H₅CO⁺ (base, 57)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: loss of C₂H₅• from either side of the symmetric carbonyl: [C₂H₅COC₂H₅]⁺• → [C₂H₅CO]⁺ + C₂H₅•. The propanoyl (acylium) cation [C₂H₅−C≡O]⁺ at m/z = 57. This peak DOES NOT APPEAR in pentan-2-one (which has m/z 43 as base peak). KEY DISTINCTION between the two isomers.

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺ (29)
peaks.explanation: COMPLEMENTARY FRAGMENT to m/z 57. The other product of α-cleavage: [C₂H₅COC₂H₅]⁺• → [C₂H₅CO]⁺ + •C₂H₅, or the ethyl cation directly. Strong at 95% — another diagnostic feature distinguishing pentan-3-one from pentan-2-one.

peaks.label: CO⁺• (28)
peaks.explanation: CO⁺• from decarbonylation of the propanoyl cation: [C₂H₅CO]⁺ → [C₂H₅]⁺ + CO, or [C₂H₅CO]⁺ → [CO]⁺• + C₂H₅. m/z = 28.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: Ethanoic Acid (Acetic Acid)
compounds.description: Ethanoic acid shows a strong molecular ion (carboxylic acids have relatively stable M⁺). The base peak at m/z 45 is the carboxyl cation [COOH]⁺ (or [CO₂H]⁺). The acetyl cation [CH₃CO]⁺ at m/z 43 is also significant. This pattern — strong m/z 45 [COOH]⁺ and m/z 17 [OH]⁺ — is diagnostic for carboxylic acids.
keyFeatures: Strong M⁺ at m/z 60 (RCOOH have stable M⁺)
Base peak m/z 45 = [COOH]⁺ — diagnostic for all carboxylic acids
Loss of OH (m/z 17) and OH₂ seen

peaks.label: M⁺ (60)
peaks.explanation: Strong molecular ion at m/z = 2(12)+4(1)+2(16) = 60. Carboxylic acids have relatively strong M⁺ peaks — the molecule is stabilized by resonance (delocalization between C=O and OH).

peaks.label: [COOH]⁺ (base, 45)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: loss of CH₃• from the molecular ion: [CH₃COOH]⁺• → [COOH]⁺ + CH₃•. The carboxyl cation [COOH]⁺ (also written [CO₂H]⁺ or [HO−C≡O]⁺) at m/z = 12+16+16+1 = 45. This m/z 45 BASE PEAK IS THE DIAGNOSTIC FINGERPRINT OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS. All carboxylic acids show this.

peaks.label: CH₃CO⁺ (43)
peaks.explanation: α-CLEAVAGE from the other side of the carbonyl: [CH₃COOH]⁺• → [CH₃CO]⁺ + OH•. The acetyl cation at m/z = 43. Interesting that both m/z 45 and m/z 43 are very strong in ethanoic acid.

peaks.label: CH₃⁺ (15)
peaks.explanation: Methyl cation from loss of [COOH]•. m/z = 15. Confirms a CH₃ group adjacent to the carboxyl.

compounds.name: Propanoic Acid
compounds.description: Propanoic acid shows the characteristic carboxylic acid base peak at m/z 45 ([COOH]⁺). The peak at m/z 29 ([C₂H₅]⁺ or [CHO]⁺) and the molecular ion at m/z 74 complete the picture. The pattern of m/z 45 base peak with molecular ion at M confirms a carboxylic acid of formula CₙH₂ₙO₂.
keyFeatures: Base peak m/z 45 = [COOH]⁺ (same as all carboxylic acids)

peaks.label: M⁺ (74)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 3(12)+6(1)+2(16) = 74. Moderate M⁺ for a carboxylic acid.

peaks.label: M−1 (73)
peaks.explanation: Loss of H• from the molecular ion. m/z = 73.

peaks.label: M−17 (57)
peaks.explanation: Loss of OH• from the molecular ion: [C₂H₅COOH]⁺• → [C₂H₅CO]⁺ + OH•. m/z = 74−17 = 57. The propanoyl acylium cation.

peaks.label: [COOH]⁺ (base, 45)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: loss of C₂H₅• from the molecular ion: [C₂H₅COOH]⁺• → [COOH]⁺ + C₂H₅•. The carboxyl cation [COOH]⁺ at m/z = 45 IS THE DIAGNOSTIC FINGERPRINT of carboxylic acids. Same peak as in ethanoic acid!

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺ (29)
peaks.explanation: Ethyl cation as the complementary fragment to [COOH]⁺: [C₂H₅COOH]⁺• → [C₂H₅]⁺ + •COOH. Or from α-cleavage on the ethyl side. m/z = 29. The strong m/z 29 confirms an ethyl group (C₂H₅−) attached to the carboxyl.

peaks.label: m/z 28
peaks.explanation: m/z 28 could be [CO]⁺• from decarbonylation or [C₂H₄]⁺• from the ethyl group losing H. m/z = 28.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: Methyl Ethanoate
compounds.description: Methyl ethanoate shows ester fragmentation. The base peak at m/z 43 ([CH₃CO]⁺, acetyl cation) arises from cleavage of the O−CH₃ bond. The peak at m/z 59 (M−15, loss of CH₃) gives the methoxy ester cation. Esters often show m/z 59 (M−15 for methyl esters) as a diagnostic peak.
keyFeatures: m/z 59 = M−15 (loss of OCH₃ radical? No: M−CH₃ = 59)

peaks.label: M⁺ (74)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 3(12)+6(1)+2(16) = 74. Relatively strong M⁺ for an ester — esters have more stable molecular ions than simple alcohols.

peaks.label: M−15 (59)
peaks.explanation: Loss of CH₃• from the methyl group on oxygen: [CH₃COOCH₃]⁺• → [CH₃COO]⁺ + CH₃•. m/z = 74−15 = 59. This is typical for methyl esters — the M−15 peak (m/z = M−15) is diagnostic.

peaks.label: CH₃CO⁺ (base, 43)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. Cleavage of the ester O−C bond (α to the carbonyl, on the alkyl oxygen side): [CH₃CO−OCH₃]⁺• → [CH₃CO]⁺ + •OCH₃. The acetyl (acylium) cation at m/z = 43. The same peak as in propanone and butanone!

peaks.label: [OCH₃]⁺ (31)
peaks.explanation: Methoxyl cation from cleavage: complementary to [CH₃CO]⁺. m/z = 16+12+3 = 31. The same peak as the [CH₂OH]⁺ in primary alcohols — but here it is a minor peak.

peaks.label: CH₃⁺ (15)
peaks.explanation: Methyl cation from further fragmentation. m/z = 15.

compounds.name: Ethyl Ethanoate
compounds.description: Ethyl ethanoate (ethyl acetate) shows ester fragmentation. The base peak at m/z 45 is the ethoxyl/ethoxycarbenium cation [OC₂H₅]⁺ or from α-cleavage. The peaks at m/z 43 ([CH₃CO]⁺) and m/z 61 (M−27) also identify the acetyl group. The M⁺ at m/z 88 is weak.
keyFeatures: m/z 61 = M−27 (loss of CH₂=CH₂ + H? or CO+H₂O)

peaks.label: M⁺ (88)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 4(12)+8(1)+2(16) = 88. Weak peak — larger esters fragment more easily.

peaks.label: M−15 (73)
peaks.explanation: Loss of CH₃• from the M⁺. m/z = 73.

peaks.label: M−27 (61)
peaks.explanation: Rearrangement product involving loss of CH₂=CH₂ (28) and gain of H, giving protonated acetic acid [CH₃COOH₂]⁺ at m/z = 61. This McLafferty-type rearrangement is common in esters.

peaks.label: m/z 45 (base)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. Cleavage of the acyl-oxygen bond: [CH₃CO−OC₂H₅]⁺• → CH₃CO• + [OC₂H₅]⁺ or [CH₃CHOH]⁺. The ion at m/z = 45 is diagnostic for ethyl esters. Note this is the same m/z as the carboxyl cation [COOH]⁺ in carboxylic acids! High-resolution MS would distinguish them.

peaks.label: CH₃CO⁺ (43)
peaks.explanation: Cleavage of the ester on the other side: [CH₃CO−OC₂H₅]⁺• → [CH₃CO]⁺ + •OC₂H₅. The acetyl cation at m/z = 43. Always seen in ethanoate esters.

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺
peaks.explanation: Ethyl cation from further fragmentation. m/z = 29.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: Ethylamine
compounds.description: Ethylamine has an ODD molecular mass (45) because it contains one nitrogen (the Nitrogen Rule). The base peak at m/z 44 (M−1, loss of H) and the strong peak at m/z 30 ([CH₂=NH₂]⁺, aminomethyl cation) are characteristic of primary amines. α-cleavage adjacent to the nitrogen gives the iminium ion.
keyFeatures: ODD M⁺ (45) → odd number of nitrogen atoms (Nitrogen Rule)
Base peak m/z 44 = M−1 (loss of H)
Amines generally show strong molecular ions

peaks.label: M⁺ (45) ODD!
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 2(12)+7(1)+14 = 45. ODD molecular mass — applies the NITROGEN RULE: an odd M⁺ indicates an ODD number of nitrogen atoms. m/z 45 confirms ONE nitrogen in this molecule.

peaks.label: M−1 (base, 44)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. Loss of H• (often from the α-carbon) gives the iminium ion [CH₃CH=NH₂]⁺. Alternatively, this is the M−1 peak via loss of H. The nitrogen lone pair stabilizes the positive charge. m/z = 44.

peaks.label: M−2 (43)
peaks.explanation: Loss of 2H (as H₂) from the molecular ion or further fragmentation. m/z = 43.

peaks.label: CH₂NH₂⁺ (30)
peaks.explanation: α-CLEAVAGE: Loss of CH₃• from [CH₃−CH−NH₂]⁺•: → [CH₂=NH₂]⁺ + CH₃•. The aminomethyl (or methyleneamine) cation [CH₂=NH₂]⁺ at m/z = 12+2+14+2 = 30 is DIAGNOSTIC for primary amines.

peaks.label: CH₂=NH⁺• (28)
peaks.explanation: Loss of H from [CH₂=NH₂]⁺ gives [CH₂=NH]⁺• at m/z = 12+2+14+1 = 29? Wait: 12+2(1)+14 = 28. The methyleneimine radical cation.

peaks.label: m/z 27
peaks.explanation: m/z 27 can be [HCN]⁺• (12+1+14 = 27) from nitrogen-containing fragments, or the vinyl cation [C₂H₃]⁺ from the carbon chain.

compounds.name: Propylamine
compounds.description: Propylamine has an odd M⁺ (59) confirming one nitrogen atom. The base peak at m/z 44 ([CH₂=NHCH₃]⁺) arises from α-cleavage losing CH₃•. This is distinct from the M−1 pattern. The peak at m/z 30 ([CH₂=NH₂]⁺) confirms a primary amine.

peaks.label: M⁺ (59) ODD
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion at m/z = 3(12)+9(1)+14 = 59. ODD mass — NITROGEN RULE confirms one nitrogen atom. M⁺ is moderate.

peaks.label: M−1 (58)
peaks.explanation: Loss of H• from the α-carbon: [C₃H₇NH₂]⁺• → [C₃H₆NH₂]⁺ + H•. m/z = 58.

peaks.label: CH₂=NHCH₃⁺ (base, 44)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. α-CLEAVAGE: loss of C₂H₅• from [CH₂−CH₂−CH₃ adjacent to NH₂]⁺•: → [CH₂=NH₂ + CH₃ chain? Actually: [CH₃CH₂CH₂NH₂]⁺• → [CH₂=NHCH₃ or CH₂=NH₂−CH₂−?] + •CH₂CH₃... The iminium ion at m/z = 44 by α-cleavage losing C₂H₅•.

peaks.label: m/z 41
peaks.explanation: m/z 41 — either a hydrocarbon or nitrogen-containing fragment. m/z = 41.

peaks.label: CH₂NH₂⁺ (30)
peaks.explanation: Aminomethyl cation [CH₂=NH₂]⁺ at m/z = 30. Same as in ethylamine and ALL primary amines. This peak at m/z 30 is a consistent diagnostic feature of primary aliphatic amines.

peaks.label: CH₂=NH⁺• (28)
peaks.explanation: Loss of H from [CH₂=NH₂]⁺ gives the methyleneimine cation. m/z = 28.

compounds.name: Chloromethane
compounds.description: Chloromethane demonstrates the characteristic M and M+2 peaks of a chlorine-containing compound. The ³⁵Cl molecular ion at m/z 50 and the ³⁷Cl molecular ion at m/z 52 appear in approximately a 3:1 ratio (reflecting the 75.5:24.5 natural abundance of ³⁵Cl:³⁷Cl). This 3:1 doublet pattern is the DIAGNOSTIC fingerprint of ONE chlorine atom.
keyFeatures: M and M+2 peaks in ~3:1 ratio (one Cl atom)

peaks.label: M⁺ ³⁵Cl (base, 50)
peaks.fragmentLabel: Chloromethane with ³⁵Cl — molecular ion (base peak, m/z 50)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK = MOLECULAR ION with ³⁵Cl. m/z = 12+3(1)+35 = 50. Natural abundance of ³⁵Cl = 75.5%, so this is the more abundant isotopologue. Assigned 100% (base peak). Note: this is M, not an M+1 carbon peak.

peaks.label: M+2 ³⁷Cl (52)
peaks.explanation: M+2 PEAK. Molecular ion with ³⁷Cl. m/z = 12+3(1)+37 = 52. Natural abundance of ³⁷Cl = 24.5%. Relative to M (³⁵Cl = 100%), this peak appears at ~32% (= 24.5/75.5 × 100 ≈ 32%). The 3:1 ratio (100:32 ≈ 3:1) is the FINGERPRINT OF ONE CHLORINE ATOM.

peaks.label: CH₃⁺ (15)
peaks.explanation: Loss of Cl• from the molecular ion: [CH₃Cl]⁺• → [CH₃]⁺ + Cl•. Both isotopologues (m/z 50 and 52) lose their chlorine, giving the same CH₃⁺ peak at m/z = 15. Only ONE peak (no isotope splitting) because the chlorine atom has been lost.

compounds.name: Chloroethane
compounds.description: Chloroethane shows the 3:1 M:M+2 chlorine isotope pattern (m/z 64 and 66). The loss of HCl (mass 36 for ³⁵Cl) gives the ethylene radical cation at m/z 28. The ethyl cation (m/z 29) is produced by loss of ³⁵Cl•.

peaks.label: M⁺ ³⁵Cl (base, 64)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK = MOLECULAR ION with ³⁵Cl. m/z = 2(12)+5(1)+35 = 64. The 3:1 doublet confirms ONE chlorine atom.

peaks.label: M+2 ³⁷Cl (66)
peaks.explanation: M+2 PEAK. Molecular ion with ³⁷Cl. m/z = 66. Approximately 1/3 the intensity of m/z 64, confirming ONE chlorine atom (3:1 ratio).

peaks.label: C₂H₅⁺ (29)
peaks.explanation: Loss of ³⁵Cl• from the [³⁵Cl] molecular ion: [C₂H₅³⁵Cl]⁺• → [C₂H₅]⁺ + ³⁵Cl•. The ethyl cation at m/z = 2(12)+5(1) = 29. Only ONE peak here (no isotope splitting) because the chlorine has been ejected as a radical.

peaks.label: C₂H₄⁺• (28)
peaks.explanation: Loss of HCl (mass = 36 for H+³⁵Cl) from M⁺: [C₂H₅³⁵Cl]⁺• → [C₂H₄]⁺• + HCl. m/z = 28. Elimination of HCl is common in halogenoalkane fragmentation.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: 1-Chloropropane
compounds.description: 1-Chloropropane shows the M:M+2 3:1 pattern for one chlorine atom at m/z 78/80. The C₃H₇⁺ cation (m/z 43) from loss of ³⁵Cl is an important peak. Compare with 2-chloropropane: same M⁺ but different fragmentation.

peaks.label: M⁺ ³⁵Cl (78)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion with ³⁵Cl. m/z = 3(12)+7(1)+35 = 78. Base peak.

peaks.label: M+2 ³⁷Cl (80)
peaks.explanation: M+2 peak with ³⁷Cl. m/z = 80. ~1/3 intensity of m/z 78, confirming one Cl atom.

peaks.label: M−15 ³⁵Cl (63)
peaks.explanation: Loss of CH₃• from the molecular ion: [CH₃CH₂CH₂³⁵Cl]⁺• → [CH₂CH₂³⁵Cl]⁺ + CH₃•. m/z = 78−15 = 63. Note this fragment still retains the Cl atom, so it also has an isotope peak (m/z 65).

peaks.label: m/z 65 (³⁷Cl)
peaks.explanation: The ³⁷Cl counterpart of the m/z 63 fragment. Ratio m/z 63:65 ≈ 3:1, confirming one Cl in this fragment ion.

peaks.label: C₃H₇⁺ (43)
peaks.fragmentLabel: Propyl cation C₃H₇⁺ (m/z 43) — loss of Cl•
peaks.explanation: Loss of ³⁵Cl• from the molecular ion: [C₃H₇³⁵Cl]⁺• → [C₃H₇]⁺ + ³⁵Cl•. The propyl cation at m/z = 3(12)+7(1) = 43. Note: only ONE peak because the Cl has been ejected.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺ (41)
peaks.explanation: Loss of HCl from C₃H₇⁺ or from M⁺. Allyl cation at m/z = 41.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation. m/z = 27.

compounds.name: 2-Chloropropane
compounds.description: 2-Chloropropane (same formula and M⁺ as 1-chloropropane!) shows a much stronger base peak at m/z 43 (isopropyl/propyl cation, loss of Cl). In 2-chloropropane, the Cl is on the secondary carbon and its loss directly produces the stable secondary carbocation (isopropyl cation, more stable than primary). The M+2 pattern at m/z 80 confirms one chlorine.
keyFeatures: MUCH stronger m/z 43 (secondary carbocation more stable)
Loss of Cl from secondary C gives stable (CH₃)₂CH⁺

peaks.label: M⁺ ³⁵Cl (78)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion with ³⁵Cl. m/z = 78. SAME as 1-chloropropane! Cannot distinguish isomers from M⁺ alone.

peaks.label: M+2 ³⁷Cl (80)
peaks.explanation: M+2 peak with ³⁷Cl. Ratio 3:1 confirms one Cl atom. Identical to 1-chloropropane's M+2 pattern.

peaks.label: M−15 ³⁵Cl (63)
peaks.explanation: Loss of CH₃• from the molecular ion. m/z = 63. Same as in 1-chloropropane but notice different relative intensities.

peaks.label: isoPropyl⁺ (base, 43)
peaks.fragmentLabel: Isopropyl cation [(CH₃)₂CH]⁺ (base peak, m/z 43) — DIFFERENT from 1-isomer!
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. Loss of ³⁵Cl• from the secondary carbon: [(CH₃)₂CH³⁵Cl]⁺• → [(CH₃)₂CH]⁺ + ³⁵Cl•. The isopropyl cation [(CH₃)₂CH]⁺ is a SECONDARY CARBOCATION — more stable than the primary propyl cation in 1-chloropropane. Hence m/z 43 is MUCH more intense here. This is the key difference between the two isomers!

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺ (41)
peaks.explanation: Loss of HCl or further fragmentation. m/z = 41.

peaks.label: C₃H₃⁺ (39)
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation. m/z = 39.

compounds.name: Bromomethane
compounds.description: Bromomethane shows the characteristic 1:1 M:M+2 doublet of ONE bromine atom (⁷⁹Br 50.7%, ⁸¹Br 49.3% — nearly equal abundances). The molecular ions at m/z 94 (with ⁷⁹Br) and m/z 96 (with ⁸¹Br) are almost equal in height. This near-equal intensity doublet is the DIAGNOSTIC PATTERN for one bromine atom.
keyFeatures: ⁷⁹Br molecular ion m/z 94 ≈ same height as ⁸¹Br m/z 96
Contrast with Cl: 3:1 Cl vs ~1:1 Br

peaks.label: M⁺ ⁷⁹Br (94)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion with ⁷⁹Br. m/z = 12+3(1)+79 = 94. ⁷⁹Br is very slightly more abundant (50.7%) than ⁸¹Br (49.3%), so this is just barely the base peak.

peaks.label: M+2 ⁸¹Br (96)
peaks.explanation: M+2 PEAK. Almost EQUAL intensity (~97%) to M⁺ at m/z 94. This near-1:1 ratio is the DIAGNOSTIC FINGERPRINT OF ONE BROMINE ATOM. Contrast with chlorine (3:1 ratio)! m/z = 96.

peaks.label: ⁷⁹Br⁺ (79)
peaks.explanation: Atomic bromine-79 cation formed by loss of CH₃• from the M⁺ ion. These Br⁺ peaks always appear as a near-equal doublet at m/z 79 and 81.

peaks.label: ⁸¹Br⁺ (81)
peaks.explanation: Atomic bromine-81 cation. Near-equal to m/z 79 peak (1:1 ratio). The presence of two near-equal Br⁺ peaks at m/z 79 and 81 confirms bromine in the molecule.

peaks.label: CH₃⁺ (15)
peaks.explanation: Loss of Br• from the molecular ion: [CH₃Br]⁺• → [CH₃]⁺ + Br•. Single peak at m/z 15 — after the bromine leaves, no isotope splitting in the methyl fragment.

compounds.name: 1-Bromopropane
compounds.description: 1-Bromopropane shows the near-equal 1:1 M:M+2 doublet at m/z 122/124 diagnostic for ONE bromine. Loss of ⁷⁹Br• gives the propyl cation (m/z 43). The ⁷⁹Br⁺/⁸¹Br⁺ doublet at 79/81 is visible. This is a good example of a larger bromine-containing molecule.

peaks.label: M⁺ ⁷⁹Br (122)
peaks.explanation: Molecular ion with ⁷⁹Br. m/z = 3(12)+7(1)+79 = 122. Moderate M⁺.

peaks.label: M+2 ⁸¹Br (124)
peaks.explanation: M+2 PEAK. Nearly equal to M⁺ (~97% relative intensity). The near-1:1 doublet at m/z 122:124 confirms ONE BROMINE ATOM.

peaks.label: ⁷⁹Br⁺ (base, 79)
peaks.fragmentLabel: ⁷⁹Br⁺ bromine atomic cation (base peak, m/z 79)
peaks.explanation: BASE PEAK. ⁷⁹Br⁺ from loss of CH₃CH₂CH₂•. m/z = 79. Large in 1-bromopropane.

peaks.label: ⁸¹Br⁺ (81)
peaks.explanation: ⁸¹Br⁺ at near-equal intensity to ⁷⁹Br⁺ (~99%). This 1:1 Br pair confirms bromine.

peaks.label: C₃H₇⁺ (43)
peaks.explanation: Loss of ⁷⁹Br• from the molecular ion: [C₃H₇⁷⁹Br]⁺• → [C₃H₇]⁺ + ⁷⁹Br•. Propyl cation at m/z = 43.

peaks.label: C₃H₅⁺ (41)
peaks.explanation: Allyl cation from further fragmentation or HBr elimination. m/z = 41.

peaks.label: C₂H₃⁺
peaks.explanation: Further fragmentation. m/z = 27.
